md: Every commercial transaction has up to four prices floating around. Get these straight and the formulas almost write themselves.
**Cost Price (CP)** — what the seller paid for the item.
**Selling Price (SP)** — what the buyer paid (= what the seller received).
**Marked Price (MP)** — the price tag (the 'list price' before any discount).
**Discount** — the reduction from MP to SP.

blocks.title: The five core formulas
blocks.body:
**1. Profit** = SP − CP   (when SP > CP)

**2. Loss** = CP − SP   (when SP < CP)

**3. Profit %** = $\dfrac{\text{Profit}}{\text{CP}} \times 100$

**4. Loss %** = $\dfrac{\text{Loss}}{\text{CP}} \times 100$

**5. Discount** = MP − SP, and **Discount %** = $\dfrac{\text{Discount}}{\text{MP}} \times 100$

**Always.** Profit/Loss percentages are computed **on the CP**. Discount percentage is computed **on the MP**. Mix these up and answers go wildly wrong.

md: If you know CP and Profit%, you can find SP:
If you know SP and Profit%, you can find CP:
**Common student mistake:** to find CP from SP at 20% profit, students subtract 20%. That's WRONG. The 20% is *of CP*, not SP. Use the formula.

blocks.title: Worked example 1
blocks.body:
A shopkeeper buys a bag at \$400 and sells it at \$500. Find profit% and SP if she had wanted 25% profit instead.

**Profit = \$100.** Profit% = $\dfrac{100}{400} \times 100 = 25\%$ (actually exactly the right amount). 

**For 25% target:** SP = $400 \times 1.25 = \$500$ — same! So she's hit her target.

If she'd wanted **40% profit:** SP = $400 \times 1.40 = \$560$.

md: **Marked Price** is the visible tag. **Selling Price** is what you actually pay after the discount.

blocks.title: Discount + profit combined
blocks.body:
A retailer's chain of logic:

**CP → MP** (markup, often a lot) **→ SP** (after discount; less than MP).

If the retailer wants 20% profit after offering a 25% discount, the markup on CP must be higher than 25%. Compute carefully:

If $CP$ → markup by $m\%$ → $MP$. Then discount $25\%$ → $SP$. Want $SP = 1.20 \cdot CP$.

$SP = MP \cdot 0.75 = CP \cdot (1 + m/100) \cdot 0.75 = 1.20 \cdot CP.$

$(1 + m/100) = \dfrac{1.20}{0.75} = 1.6.$ So $m = 60\%$. The MP must be 60% above CP to yield 20% profit after a 25% discount.

md: Two discounts of $a\%$ and $b\%$ applied successively do **NOT** equal a single $(a+b)\%$ discount.
If MP is \$100, first discount 20% → \$80. Second discount 10% on \$80 → \$72.
Stores use this trick to make discounts sound bigger than they are.

blocks.title: Worked example 2
blocks.body:
A book is marked at \$500. It is sold at successive discounts of 10% and 5%. Find SP.

**Method 1 (direct):** $500 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = \$427.50$.

**Method 2 (single equivalent):** Equivalent single discount = $10 + 5 - \dfrac{50}{100} = 14.5\%$. So SP = $500 \times 0.855 = \$427.50$. ✓

md: **Example.** A trader sells two TVs for \$15000 each. On one he gains 25% and on the other he loses 25%. What's the net result?
**Lesson:** equal-and-opposite percentage profit/loss does NOT cancel out, because the percentages are taken on different CPs. Always **work with the underlying rupees**, not the percentages.
